ontology.foodex2_description: Bread and similar products — toasted bread
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated toast code; A000F (bread and similar products) is the closest defensible parent category. Toasted white bread is a processed form of white bread.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard slice of white toast weighs approximately 30 g and is typically consumed at breakfast, often with butter, kaya, or jam.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore HPB Foodo — Toast (white bread, toasted)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for toasted white bread (F05030022). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — White bread, toasted
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 73 (±0) for white bread toasted is reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI table (Am J Clin Nutr). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 73 × 16.35 / 100 ≈ 11.9, based on a 30 g serving containing 16.35 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 537 mg per 100 g with low potassium offset (131 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit white toast intake due to its high sodium content (537 mg/100 g); choose low-sodium bread varieties and avoid adding salty spreads such as salted butter or kaya to prevent further sodium loading.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (73), high carbohydrate (54.5 g/100 g), low dietary fibre (2.9 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: White toast has a high glycemic index of 73 and delivers 54.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g with only 2.9 g fibre, causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with type 2 diabetes should substitute with wholemeal or multigrain toast and pair with protein to blunt glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (537 mg/100 g) and refined carbohydrate load; low saturated fat (0.9 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: White toast contributes significant sodium (537 mg/100 g) and refined carbohydrate that may adversely affect blood pressure and triglyceride levels in individuals with cardiovascular disease; limit portion size, choose wholegrain alternatives, and avoid high-fat or high-salt toppings.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate with high glycaemic load and absence of anti-inflammatory nutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: White toast provides a high glycaemic load that may promote pro-inflammatory mediators; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute with wholemeal or sourdough bread and avoid adding butter or margarine that contribute saturated or trans fats.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Toast is white bread that has been subjected to dry heat via a toaster or grill, producing a browned, crisp exterior through the Maillard reaction while the interior remains slightly chewy. It is one of the most widely consumed breakfast staples globally and is a common component of the Singaporean breakfast table, often served with kaya (coconut-egg jam) and butter, or with soft-boiled eggs and soy sauce. Per 100 g, white toast provides 290 kcal, 9 g protein, 4 g fat, and 54.5 g carbohydrate with only 2.9 g dietary fibre, making it a high-carbohydrate, low-fibre food. Its sodium content of 537 mg/100 g is notable, arising from salt added during bread manufacture. The glycemic index of white toast is approximately 73 (high GI), and a standard 30 g slice delivers a glycemic load of approximately 11.9. Clinically, white toast is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to its high GI and low fibre, and is a concern for hypertension management due to its sodium content. It is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-compatible in its plain form, but contains gluten and is not suitable for coeliac disease or gluten intolerance. Wholemeal or multigrain alternatives are consistently recommended as nutritionally superior substitutes.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a slice of toast?
Can I eat toast if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of white toast?
Is toast suitable for weight loss?
What is the difference between toast and bread nutritionally?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F05030022) at confidence 0.95. GI value of 73 for white toast is well-established in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Source match is exact for both nutrient and GI data.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific HPB toast entry (F05030022) are not reported in the HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat estimated at 0.9 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for white bread toasted (NDB 18023), which is the closest approved-source analogue; this value is documented as an adaptation. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GI (73) × available carbohydrate per 30 g serving (16.35 g) / 100 = 11.9. Toast is a generic Western food item; no Singaporean-specific multilingual naming is required as this is not a Singaporean dish — it is an internationally generic staple consumed across all cuisines. The food_name 'Toast' is the canonical English name and no mandatory local-language names apply under G6 multilingual rules for this cuisine category.
